caveats: a: OEWS aggregates all NAICS 423100 businesses, not tire wholesalers alone.
a: Occupation shares are neither wage-weighted task exposure nor measures of current automation.
a: Passenger replacement, commercial fleet, industrial, agricultural, and used-tire distributors have different workflows.
rho: No public study measures five-year AI implementation for 423130.
rho: Safety-sensitive fitment and load-rating decisions require reliable data and accountable review.
rho: Smaller route distributors may have limited integration capacity and sparse clean histories.
e: Public data do not measure eligibility among LMM firms.
e: The broader 4231 manufacturer-branch share may differ materially for tires.
e: The dataset n is margin-bridged rather than an observed EBITDA-band count.
s5: No public 423130 control-transfer series was found.
s5: Broker listings and announced transactions are biased toward larger or sale-ready companies.
s5: Closures, internal reorganizations, and asset-only liquidations are not qualifying transfers.
q: No observed study measures retention of AI-generated benefit in tire wholesaling.
q: Commodity price and manufacturer rebate changes can obscure operating benefit.
q: Capture should be lower for transparent passenger SKUs and higher for scarce commercial or specialty applications.
d5: USTMA is a manufacturer association, and its forecast is not an independent wholesale-channel forecast.
d5: Shipment figures are units, while d5 is constant-price, constant-quality demand for the current service basket.
d5: Tariffs, imports, freight, fleet cycles, tire longevity, and direct manufacturer distribution can change wholesaler demand independently of miles driven.
o: Accountable physical functions could migrate to manufacturers, platforms, or third-party logistics providers rather than disappear.
o: The broad OEWS physical-job shares may not match 423130.
o: Digital fitment standards and platform guarantees could reduce the independent operator role faster than expected.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B customers purchase replacement or original-equipment tires and tubes; distributors earn product margin and may provide assortment availability, fitment support, warehousing, route delivery, emergency fulfillment, credit, returns, warranty, casing, and recall administration.
lens.excluded_activities: Manufacturer-owned or captive sales branches, commission agents and electronic marketplaces, tire manufacturing and retreading, retail-only tire dealers, scrap processors, shells, internal reorganizations, and firms without transferable recurring accounts.
lens.included_activities: Independent US merchant distributors and jobbers that repeatedly source, stock, sell, and deliver new or used tires and tubes for passenger, light-truck, commercial-truck, bus, industrial, off-road, farm, and other vehicles to dealers, installers, fleets, retailers, and business users.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations and wages for broad NAICS 423100 rather than wage-weighted tasks in 423130. I map parts of quoting, account service, fitment search, inventory records, purchasing, pricing, order entry, claims, and dispatch to AI exposure, while treating driving, tire handling, warehouse work, inspection, and relationship negotiation as mostly unexposed. The four-digit mix may overstate exposure if tire wholesalers are more physical and route-delivery intensive than other motor-vehicle distributors.
caveats: OEWS aggregates all NAICS 423100 businesses, not tire wholesalers alone.
Occupation shares are neither wage-weighted task exposure nor measures of current automation.
Passenger replacement, commercial fleet, industrial, agricultural, and used-tire distributors have different workflows.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task study across 15-20 independent tire wholesalers, measuring time and wages in quoting, fitment, purchasing, pricing, customer service, warehouse, delivery, claims, returns, credit, and work already automated.
a.rationale: The 423100 proxy has 19.66% of employment in sales and 13.83% in office/administrative support, including customer service, orders, bookkeeping, shipping records, and general office work. Tire size, application, availability, pricing, and replenishment data create structured AI-assistance opportunities. Yet transportation/material-moving is 29.96% and installation/repair 16.10% of the broad workforce, and bulky physical inventory, loading, delivery, condition, and account responsibility remain labor-bound.

d5.bridge: USTMA reports US tire manufacturer shipment history and a one-year forecast, NHTSA reports annual replacement-tire purchases, and FHWA reports vehicles and miles; none measures five-year constant-price demand through independent 423130 wholesalers. I treat stable-to-rising shipments, more than 300 million vehicles, roughly 340 million annual replacement purchases, and 3.294 trillion 2024 vehicle-miles as unit-demand anchors, then allow for wholesaler channel loss, product durability, economic cycles, and mix. Manufacturer shipments may overstate distributor demand where sales go direct and understate it where imports flow through wholesalers.
caveats: USTMA is a manufacturer association, and its forecast is not an independent wholesale-channel forecast.
Shipment figures are units, while d5 is constant-price, constant-quality demand for the current service basket.
Tariffs, imports, freight, fleet cycles, tire longevity, and direct manufacturer distribution can change wholesaler demand independently of miles driven.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year unit forecast by passenger, light-truck, commercial, off-road, agricultural, and industrial segment, split between replacement and OE and adjusted for imports, retreads, tire life, vehicle miles, and the independent-wholesale channel share.
d5.rationale: Tire wear, damage, aging, and safety replacement create recurring physical demand. NHTSA says US owners buy approximately 340 million replacement tires annually, and USTMA projected total shipments of 340.2 million in 2025 versus 337.3 million in 2024 and 332.7 million in 2019; replacement passenger, light-truck, and truck shipments were all forecast to grow in 2025. FHWA reports vehicle miles above 2019. These signals support modest five-year real growth, not rapid expansion.

caveats: Public data do not measure eligibility among LMM firms.
The broader 4231 manufacturer-branch share may differ materially for tires.
The dataset n is margin-bridged rather than an observed EBITDA-band count.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the dataset-estimated LMM population tagged by ultimate ownership, merchant versus agent status, repeat-order share, route density, customer mix, concentration, employee depth, and owner dependence, verified through operator interviews.
e.rationale: Tires are repeatedly replaced, and independent distributors commonly serve recurring dealer, installer, retailer, and fleet accounts through inventory availability and physical delivery. Census identifies merchant wholesale distributors and jobbers buying and selling on their own account, alongside manufacturer branches and nonmerchant intermediaries that the lens excludes. Eligibility is also reduced by one-owner trading operations, captive territories, concentration, and businesses without transferable service infrastructure.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 423130 ($2,509,013,244) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 423130 ($58,087,065,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1137 (margins.json[42]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Distributors) EBITDA/Sales 11.37%); revenue/employee $1,338,844 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 423130); class <5: 502 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,690,622 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $192,224 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 161 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,807,627 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $546,627 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 140 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,906,586 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,012,679 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 204 firms, avg revenue/firm $33,021,799 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,754,579 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 73 firms, avg revenue/firm $87,266,014 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,922,146 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 50 firms, avg revenue/firm $842,211,140 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $95,759,407 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 417

caveats: Accountable physical functions could migrate to manufacturers, platforms, or third-party logistics providers rather than disappear.
The broad OEWS physical-job shares may not match 423130.
Digital fitment standards and platform guarantees could reduce the independent operator role faster than expected.
o.change_evidence: Channel-share data for tire units fulfilled manufacturer-direct or platform-direct without an independent distributor, plus evidence on autonomous warehouses, outsourced delivery, self-service fitment accuracy, claims, emergency fill, and fleet service levels.
o.rationale: Most year-five tire-distribution volume still requires an accountable operator to hold bulky inventory, assure size and load fitment, allocate scarce stock, arrange rapid delivery, manage credit, handle recalls, returns, warranties and casings, and resolve service exceptions. Ordering and matching can become self-service and manufacturers or marketplaces can bypass some distributors, but software alone cannot move the tire or bear inventory, fitment, and service-level responsibility.

caveats: No observed study measures retention of AI-generated benefit in tire wholesaling.
Commodity price and manufacturer rebate changes can obscure operating benefit.
Capture should be lower for transparent passenger SKUs and higher for scarce commercial or specialty applications.
q.change_evidence: Customer-SKU margin and retention panels before and after workflow automation, including net price, rebates, freight, claims, returns, inventory turns, route cost, fill rate, bid outcomes, and competitor quotes by segment.
q.rationale: An own-account distributor can initially retain AI-enabled savings through fewer service touches, better purchasing and replenishment, lower error and claim cost, and denser delivery planning. Standardized tire specifications, brand and manufacturer programs, visible online prices, fleet bids, dealer bargaining, and low switching friction make pass-through and competitive repricing powerful. Emergency availability, specialty sizes, route service, credit, and dependable warranty handling preserve some capture.

caveats: No public study measures five-year AI implementation for 423130.
Safety-sensitive fitment and load-rating decisions require reliable data and accountable review.
Smaller route distributors may have limited integration capacity and sparse clean histories.
rho.change_evidence: Twelve-month operating studies from tire distributors using AI-enabled ERP, CRM, fitment, pricing, forecasting, and customer-service systems, reporting adoption, override and wrong-fit rates, labor hours, stockouts, claims, customer retention, and implementation cost.
rho.rationale: Within five years, distributors can plausibly deploy AI in fitment and substitution suggestions, quote generation, demand and reorder forecasting, price monitoring, account prioritization, service triage, proof-of-delivery processing, and warranty or return classification. Online wholesale and AI-enabled selling are already recognized by BLS, but legacy ERP and catalog data, safety and fitment liability, route constraints, supplier programs, customer habits, and exception-heavy commercial tire service constrain implementation.

caveats: No public 423130 control-transfer series was found.
Broker listings and announced transactions are biased toward larger or sale-ready companies.
Closures, internal reorganizations, and asset-only liquidations are not qualifying transfers.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed tire-distributor transactions from industry brokers and deal databases, coded for control and operating continuity, combined with a representative owner survey on age, successor availability, planned exit date, and willingness to sell.
s5.rationale: A one-in-five five-year qualifying control-transfer probability is a bounded judgment for independent, transferable tire distributors. Succession, retirement, regional route density, manufacturer relationships, and distributor consolidation can motivate sales, but many owners hold, transfer internally, close, or sell inventory and customer lists without a qualifying operating-company transfer.

principal_driver: Recurring safety- and wear-driven replacement creates repeat inventory, fitment, fulfillment, and account workflows that AI can assist.
principal_weakness: Low labor intensity and standardized, visible product pricing constrain the amount of implemented benefit that a distributor can retain.

sources.supports: Identifies new and used tire-and-tube wholesaling for passenger and commercial vehicles; distinguishes own-account distributors, manufacturer branches, and agents; asks about inventories, e-commerce, customer classes, selling methods, employee functions, and automation technologies.
sources.title: 2022 Economic Census Form: Tire and Tube Merchant Wholesalers

sources.supports: Reports 367,580 total jobs; 19.66% sales, 13.83% office/administrative support, 16.10% installation/maintenance/repair, and 29.96% transportation/material-moving employment, with detailed occupations and wages.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 423100

sources.supports: Describes prospecting, needs analysis, pricing, negotiation, contracts, orders, follow-up, reports, and online selling; says online wholesale mostly complements face-to-face sales and AI/chatbots may limit employment growth.
sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Wholesale and Manufacturing Sales Representatives

sources.supports: States that worn, damaged, irregularly worn, and aging tires require replacement; reports more than 300 million US vehicles in 2024 and approximately 340 million replacement tires purchased annually.
sources.title: NHTSA TireWise: Tire Safety Ratings and Awareness

sources.supports: Projects 340.2 million US tire shipments in 2025 versus 337.3 million in 2024 and 332.7 million in 2019; forecasts 2025 replacement passenger, light-truck, and truck shipments up 1.2%, 2.5%, and 3.7%.
sources.title: USTMA July 2025 Forecast Predicts Higher 2025 Tire Shipments

sources.supports: Defines the broader 4231 merchant model and reports manufacturer sales branches represented 49.9% of group sales while merchant wholesalers excluding those branches represented 50.1%, confirming a material captive component.
sources.title: Motor Vehicle and Motor Vehicle Parts and Supplies Merchant Wholesalers: 2022

sources.supports: Reports national annual vehicle-miles of 3.294 trillion in 2024, above 3.262 trillion in 2019 and the 2020 trough.
sources.title: FHWA Highway Statistics 2024: Annual Vehicle-Miles of Travel, 1980-2024

title: Tire and Tube Merchant Wholesalers
